description: Arecaceae is the palm family, comprising over 2,600 species of trees, shrubs, and vines. Economic palms are those cultivated for food, fibre, oil, and other commercial products. India has a rich palm culture with species integral to agriculture, cuisine, and tradition across its diverse regional landscapes.
meta_title: Economic Palms of India — Arecaceae | PhytoCommerce
meta_description: Explore economic palms of India including Coconut, Date Palm, Betel Nut, Palmyra and more. Full botanical data with Indian regional names and cultivar listings.
meta_keywords: economic palms India, coconut palm, date palm, betel nut, palmyra palm, Arecaceae India

genera.description: Cocos nucifera, the coconut palm, is one of the most economically important palms in the world and a cornerstone of Indian coastal agriculture. Known as Nariyal in Hindi, it is called Narikela in Sanskrit, Thengai in Tamil, Kobbari in Telugu, and Thengu in Malayalam. Every part of the plant — from fruit and oil to coir fibre and timber — is utilised. India is one of the top three global producers of coconuts.

species.description: Cocos nucifera is a tall monocotyledonous palm reaching 30 m, with a distinctive arching crown of pinnate leaves. The drupe (coconut) is consumed fresh, dried (copra), pressed for oil, or processed into various food products. Coir from the husk is a major fibre crop. Coconut water is a traditional beverage and sports drink.
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Cocos nucifera (Coconut/Nariyal). Tall, West Coast and East Coast cultivars, D×T hybrids, copra and oil statistics for Indian growers.
species.meta_keywords: Cocos nucifera, coconut palm, Nariyal, Indian coconut cultivars, West Coast Tall, D×T hybrid
species.notes: Grown commercially along India's coastal states. Requires well-drained sandy loam and high humidity.

genera.description: Phoenix is a genus of 14 species of palms native to regions spanning the Canary Islands through Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia. The genus is best known for P. dactylifera (Date Palm), though India has important native wild date species. Phoenix palms are dioecious, requiring both male and female plants for fruiting. The genus name is ancient Greek, possibly referencing Phoenicia or the mythical bird.

species.description: Phoenix dactylifera is the commercially cultivated date palm, one of humanity's oldest food crops. In India it is grown primarily in Rajasthan and Gujarat. The sweet drupes (dates) are consumed fresh, dried, or processed. Cultivation requires intense heat, low humidity at fruit maturity, and irrigation in arid regions.
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Phoenix dactylifera (Date Palm/Khajoor). Commercial cultivation in Rajasthan and Gujarat; cultivar notes and production data.
species.meta_keywords: Phoenix dactylifera, date palm, Khajoor, Indian dates, Rajasthan dates
species.notes: Requires high heat and dry conditions at fruiting. Predominantly grown in arid NW India.

species.description: Phoenix sylvestris, the wild date palm, is a native Indian species distributed widely across the subcontinent. Known as Khajoor Junglee in Hindi and Eetha in Telugu. The sap is tapped for toddy and jaggery (date palm sugar/Nolen Gur), a Bengal winter delicacy. The tree plays an important role in rural economies and is a prominent landscape palm.
species.meta_title: Phoenix sylvestris — Wild Date Palm / Khajoor Junglee
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Phoenix sylvestris, the Indian wild date palm. Tapped for toddy and Nolen Gur (date palm jaggery); widely distributed native species.
species.meta_keywords: Phoenix sylvestris, wild date palm, Khajoor Junglee, Nolen Gur, date palm jaggery India
species.native_range: Indian subcontinent (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Sri Lanka)
species.notes: Native to India. Sap tapped in winter for toddy and jaggery. Drought tolerant once established.

species.description: Phoenix rupicola, the cliff date palm, is a graceful species native to rocky outcrops and hillsides in eastern India, particularly Sikkim, Bhutan, and the eastern Himalayas. Known as Pahadi Khajoor (Hill Date Palm). It is smaller than P. sylvestris, with soft arching leaves and small edible fruits. Increasingly popular as an ornamental in Indian horticulture.
species.meta_title: Phoenix rupicola — Cliff Date Palm / Pahadi Khajoor
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Phoenix rupicola (Cliff Date/Pahadi Khajoor). Native Indian palm from rocky hillsides of the eastern Himalayas; ornamental and edible.
species.meta_keywords: Phoenix rupicola, cliff date palm, Pahadi Khajoor, Indian native palm, eastern Himalaya palm
species.notes: Rocky hillside native. Drought hardy. Soft, graceful fronds; suitable for garden landscapes.

genera.description: Areca comprises about 50 species of palms native to tropical Asia and the Pacific. Areca catechu, the betel nut palm, is of immense cultural and economic significance across South and Southeast Asia. The areca nut (commonly but incorrectly called betel nut) is chewed with betel leaf (Piper betle) and slaked lime as a mild stimulant in a preparation called paan. India is a major producer.

species.description: Areca catechu is a slender, elegant palm reaching 20–30 m. Cultivated extensively in Karnataka, Kerala, Assam, and other humid regions. The nut is chewed as paan (Paan Supari / Tambool), an ancient South Asian tradition. Regional names include Adike in Kannada and Kammugu in Tamil. The palm also yields husks used for fibre and packaging materials.
species.meta_title: Areca catechu — Betel Nut Palm / Supari
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Areca catechu (Betel Nut/Supari). Cultural and economic significance of paan in India; cultivation in Karnataka, Kerala, Assam.
species.meta_keywords: Areca catechu, betel nut palm, Supari, paan, Adike, Kammugu, Indian areca cultivation
species.native_range: Unknown wild origin (possibly Philippines/Malaysia); cultivated across tropical Asia
species.notes: Requires humid tropical climate. Known as Paan Supari (Hindi), Tambool (Sanskrit), Adike (Kannada), Kammugu (Tamil).

genera.description: Borassus is a genus of five species of tall dioecious fan palms native to tropical Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia. Borassus flabellifer is the dominant species in India, known by many regional names and deeply embedded in Indian culture. Every part — fruit, sap, flower spathe, seeds, and leaves — is utilised, earning it the title 'Tree of a Thousand Uses' in Tamil Nadu.

species.description: Borassus flabellifer is a massive fan palm reaching 30 m, iconic in the flat plains of Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and West Bengal. It is the State tree of Tamil Nadu. The palm sap is fermented into toddy or concentrated into jaggery (Taal Mishri). The young endosperm (Nungu/Ice Apple) is a popular summer cooling food. Regional names: Taal (Hindi), Nungu (Tamil), Taati (Telugu), Tal (Bengali).
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Borassus flabellifer (Palmyra/Taal). State tree of Tamil Nadu; jaggery, toddy, Ice Apple (Nungu) and fibre uses across India.
species.meta_keywords: Borassus flabellifer, Palmyra palm, Taal, Nungu, Taati, Tal, ice apple, Indian palm tree
species.native_range: Tropical Africa and Asia; widely naturalised across South and Southeast Asia
species.notes: Taal (Hindi), Nungu (Tamil), Taati (Telugu), Tal (Bengali). State tree of Tamil Nadu. Multipurpose.

genera.description: Caryota is a genus of 13 species of palms notable for their unique bipinnate (twice-divided) leaves, which give the leaflets a fish-tail outline. Native to tropical and subtropical Asia and Australia. Caryota urens is the most economically important species in India, tapped for sap to produce toddy and jaggery.

species.description: Caryota urens, the toddy palm or fishtail wine palm, is a tall monocarpic palm (flowers once then dies) native to India and Sri Lanka. Known as Shendi or Sago Palm in India. The sap (toddy) is tapped from the inflorescence and consumed fresh (neera) or fermented. Starch from the trunk is used as sago. The fruits contain oxalate crystals and must not be handled bare-handed.
species.meta_title: Caryota urens — Toddy Palm / Shendi (Fish-tail Palm)
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Caryota urens (Toddy Palm/Shendi). Monocarpic Indian palm tapped for toddy and sago; distinctive bipinnate fronds.
species.meta_keywords: Caryota urens, toddy palm, Shendi, fishtail palm, Indian sago palm, neera
species.notes: Monocarpic — dies after flowering. Fruits contain calcium oxalate; handle with gloves. Sap: neera (fresh) or toddy (fermented).

genera.description: Nypa is a monotypic genus containing only Nypa fruticans, an unusual mangrove palm that grows in tidal estuaries and coastal swamps. It is considered one of the most ancient flowering plants. In India it is found in the Sundarbans mangrove delta of West Bengal and in Andaman and Nicobar Islands. The leaves are used for thatching and the sap for sugar and vinegar.

species.description: Nypa fruticans is a stemless or creeping mangrove palm with large, erect pinnate fronds rising directly from a buried rhizome. Known as Golpata in Bengali and Hogla in parts of India. Leaves are a primary thatching material in the Sundarbans. The sap is tapped for sugar and vinegar. Fruits are edible when young. The palm plays a significant role in mangrove ecosystem stability.
species.meta_description: Botanical profile of Nypa fruticans (Nipa Palm/Golpata). Mangrove palm of the Sundarbans; thatching, sugar sap, and ecosystem roles.
species.meta_keywords: Nypa fruticans, nipa palm, Golpata, Sundarbans palm, mangrove palm India, thatching palm
species.native_range: South and Southeast Asia, western Pacific (mangrove zones)
species.notes: Golpata (Bengali), Hogla. Mangrove habitat; requires saline tidal conditions. Ancient lineage — fossil record 70 Ma.
